disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Sudden onset over the past four hours of severe, unremitting muscle spasm and rigidity in one leg in a child with known cerebral palsy, far worse than his usual baseline stiffness
trigger_question_examples: What has changed today compared with how he usually is?
When did the spasms start and how bad are they?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It came on suddenly about four hours ago. He always has some stiffness from his cerebral palsy, but this is completely different — his leg is locked rigid and he's screaming in pain.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The affected leg is held locked straight with the foot pointing down; any passive movement or touch triggers severe pain and a whole-body startle
trigger_question_examples: Which part of his body is affected and what does it look like?
What happens when you try to move the leg?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It's his right leg — the knee is locked out straight and his foot points down. If we so much as touch it he screams and his whole body jolts.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: His regular oral baclofen was given about two hours ago with no effect on the spasm or pain
trigger_question_examples: What medicines does he take and have you given anything today?
Has his usual muscle-relaxing medicine helped at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We gave him his usual liquid baclofen about two hours ago and it hasn't made any difference at all.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: He cannot be fed at present because of the pain and distress; baseline drooling and slurred speech are unchanged
trigger_question_examples: Is he able to eat or drink right now?
Is his drooling or speech any different from usual?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We can't feed him at all right now, he's too distressed. His drooling and slurred speech are just how he always is, no different.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No witnessed fall, knock, or injury before the spasms began, but he cannot say himself whether anything hurt him
trigger_question_examples: Did he have any fall, knock or injury before this started?
Could he have hurt his leg or hip that you might not have seen?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We didn't see him fall or hurt himself, but honestly he can't tell us if something hurt — he doesn't talk much and just cries when he's in pain.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I don't think he fell... but I can't be certain, we're not with him every second.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Screening negatives for a treatable trigger: normal bowel motion this morning, tummy soft and not swollen, no fever felt, no new pressure sores seen
trigger_question_examples: When did he last open his bowels and is his tummy soft?
Does he feel hot, or are there any new red areas or sores on his skin?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He had a normal poo this morning and his tummy feels soft. He doesn't feel hot to us, and we haven't noticed any new sores.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Currently no breathing difficulty, no blue colour, no reduced responsiveness, and no seizure — the child is alert, crying and breathing
trigger_question_examples: Is he having any trouble breathing or has he gone blue?
Is he still alert, and has he had any seizure or become unresponsive?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, he's breathing fine and he's very much awake — crying the whole time. No fits, and his colour is normal, he's just red-faced from crying.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The affected leg is not cold, pale, or mottled and the parents can feel it is warm — no sign of the limb losing its blood supply
trigger_question_examples: Is the leg or foot cold, pale or a different colour than the other side?
Does the foot still feel warm?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, the leg is still warm and looks a normal colour, same as the other one — it's just locked stiff.
